institutions.institution_blurb: Phillips Academy Andover. Yale 1864; Columbia Law 1866.
Donated $150,000 to The Taft School in 1929; CPT building named for him.
Co-founded with wife Anna Sinton; bequeathed house and 690 artworks to Cincinnati.

cross_connections.display_label: His wife Anna Sinton's father David Sinton left her $20 million — Ohio's largest fortune in 1900. Sinton money financed the presidential bid.

notable.notable_blurb: Owned the Chicago Cubs and Philadelphia Phillies

narrative_blocks.header: He owned the Cubs and the Phillies
narrative_blocks.body: Charles Phelps Taft became a minority owner of the Chicago Cubs in 1905 and helped fund the purchase of the Philadelphia Phillies in 1909 — publicly denying the connection until it became untenable. After serving one term as a congressman and building the Cincinnati Times-Star into the foundation of a media empire, his greatest business legacy was baseball. He sold the Cubs after the 1915 season and his estate, the Taft Museum of Art, is now open to the public.

narrative_blocks.header: His half-brother accepted the presidential nomination
narrative_blocks.body: William Howard Taft accepted the 1908 Republican presidential nomination standing on the portico of Charles Phelps Taft's Cincinnati home at 316 Pike Street — the house their father-in-law David Sinton had purchased and that Charles and Anna later bequeathed to the public as the Taft Museum of Art. The nomination scene on that portico is one of the moments that cement the house's place in American political history.

narrative_blocks.header: She donated $5 million to the University
narrative_blocks.body: Anna Sinton Taft inherited $20 million from her father David Sinton and spent much of her life building one of the most significant private art collections in the American West. In 1927, she and Charles Phelps Taft bequeathed their home and 690 artworks to the people of Cincinnati. The year after he died in 1929, she donated $5 million to the University of Cincinnati, establishing what became the Charles Phelps Taft Research Center.

institutions.institution_blurb: Co-founded with husband Charles Phelps Taft; bequeathed house and 690 artworks to Cincinnati.

narrative_blocks.body: Alphonso Taft co-founded Skull and Bones at Yale in 1832 with William Huntington Russell — who was also a Hooker descendant, though neither man knew it. His son William Howard Taft joined the same society, as did Henry Waters Taft and Horace Dutton Taft. Alphonso served as Attorney General, Secretary of War, and U.S. Minister to both Austria-Hungary and Russia — but his greatest legacy was the political dynasty: William became the only person to hold both the presidency and the Chief Justiceship.

narrative_blocks.header: He dissented in the Bible-in-schools case
narrative_blocks.body: In 1872, Alphonso Taft dissented from his own court's ruling requiring Bible reading in Cincinnati's public schools. He argued that the government must be neutral toward all religions, referencing Jewish taxpayers' right to secular education. The opinion was widely admired but killed his 1875 bid for the Republican nomination for Governor of Ohio.

narrative_blocks.header: His father Peter Rawson honored Hooker ancestry
narrative_blocks.body: His father was Peter Rawson Taft — the middle name "Rawson" an explicit tribute to Peter's mother Rhoda Rawson Taft, who descended from Rev. Grindal Rawson and Susanna Wilson Rawson, herself a daughter of Rev. Thomas Hooker's daughter Sarah Hooker Wilson. The name traveled three generations to Alphonso's father before reaching him.

cross_connections.display_label: Co-founded Skull and Bones at Yale in 1832 — both were Thomas Hooker descendants, though neither knew it.
Ohio Republican Party founding-era figures — lawyer-jurist and newspaper editor.

education.notes: — Co-founder of Skull and Bones 1832 with William Huntington Russell. Father of President William Howard Taft. Hooker descendant.

narrative_blocks.header: She died twenty days before her last child
narrative_blocks.body: Fanny Phelps Taft died on June 2, 1852. Her last child, also named Alphonso, had been born in December 1851 and died on June 22, 1852 — twenty days after his mother. Of her five children, three died in infancy.

parents.father_research_notes: John Sinton, linen manufacturer, Unshinagh, County Armagh

cross_connections.display_label: His daughter Anna Sinton married Charles Phelps Taft; he left her $20 million — Ohio's largest fortune in 1900.

narrative_blocks.header: He stockpiled pig-iron before the Civil War
narrative_blocks.body: David Sinton arrived in America from County Armagh, Ireland at age three, left home at thirteen as a salesman on four dollars a month, and eventually took over the management of an Ohio ironworks. His fortune was made by stockpiling pig-iron in anticipation of the Civil War and selling at inflated wartime prices. When he died in 1900, his estate was valued at $20 million — the largest in Ohio.

narrative_blocks.header: Cincinnati mansion became the Taft Museum of Art
narrative_blocks.body: Sinton purchased the old Longworth mansion at 316 Pike Street in Cincinnati — the oldest domestic wooden structure in downtown Cincinnati, built in 1820. His daughter Anna inherited it when he died, and she lived there with her husband Charles Phelps Taft until their deaths in 1931 and 1929. In 1927 the Tafts bequeathed the house and their collection of 690 artworks to the people of Cincinnati.

institutions.institution_blurb: His Pike Street mansion — purchased 1850s, later bequeathed by his daughter as the museum.
